content.full_text:
California
Fair POlitical
Practices Cornmission
Uay 15, L99 O
Thomas S. Knox
Greve, Clifford, Diepenbrock & Paras
1000 G Street, Suite 4oO
Post office Box 2469
Sacramento, CA 95412-2469
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-90-284
Dear Mr. Knox:
This is in response to your letter requesting advice on
behalf of the nenbers of the california-Nevada Super Speed Commj.s-
sion ('rsuper Speed conmission'r) concerning the nembers' filing 
and
discLosure obligations under the Political Reform Act (the
"Act" ) . 1
This letter is a follow-uP to telephone advj,ce provided to
you on April 19, 1990.
OUESTIONS
1. If third parties paid the commi.ssioners' travel and ac-
connodation expenses incurred while travelinq in Europe to inspect
other high speed train systems, which, if any, of these payments
are reportable by the cornmissioners as incoroe or gifts?
2. Assuning that the pa)rments are reportable, must
disclosure statements be f il,ed by all- of the california cornrois-
sioners or merely by the Cal.ifornia commissioners $rho are elected
off icers?
,t' 
Governnent Code Sections 81000-91015. AII statutory references
are to the covernment code unless other\rise indicated. 
Cornmission
regulations appear at 2 california code of Regulations Section
18ooo, et seq. A11 references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 5 of the California code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 8OO . P.O. Box 8O7 . Sacramento CA 958O4-O8O7 o (916)322.5660


Our File No. A-90-284
Page 2
CONCLUSIONS
I. 
The commissioners must report the payments for travel and
accommodation expenses from Bechtel as gifts. 
The payments from
Transrapid, Al-sthon, and the government of France are gifts to the
Super Speed Commission rather tfran to the commissioners.
2. The California commissioners who are elected state offic-
ers or srho are specified in Section 87200 nust report the gifts on
the Statenents of Economic Interest which they currently fi1e.
other california commissioners who are designated in the conf Ij-ct
of interest code to be adopted by the super sPeed Coumission are
ttdesignated employeesrr within the meaning of the Act. 
Such
designated employees must disclose reportable interests, including
gifts of $5o or more, on the Stateuents of Economic Interest they
file pursuant to the agency's conflict of interest code. Those
statements must be filed within 30 days of the effective date of
the code.
FACTS
In July and Augrust of 1989, meDbers of the Super Speed com-
nission traveled to Europe to inspect hi.gh speed train systerns.
Funding for the trj.p was provided by the following:
1. Transrapid fnternational (ttTransrapidtt) , which is
constructing a high speed train in Germany;
2. Bechtel International,
a consortium with Transrapid to
proj ect ;
Inc. ( ttBechtel-r') , which has f ormed
bid on the Cal i fornia-Nevada
3. The French qovernment; and
4. Alsthom, a French manufacturer ldhich also hopes to bid on
the cal ifornia-Nevada Project.
Transrapid had donated funds to the city of Las vegas several
years ago for use in developing a high speed rail systen. The
city of Las Vegas used those funds to pay certain start-up costs
for the Super Speed Conmission. Once the Super Speed Commissi.on
was established, the city of Las vegas uade a gift of those funds
to the commission.
Alsthom and the French government did not pay money directly
to the super speed commission. They paid expenses incurred by the
comrnission in France, including the cost of hotels, tours, meals
and ground transportation.
Bechtel paid the costs of hotels, meals and ground
transportation in England.
The Super Speed Commission adopted resolutions designed to
comply with the decision in In re Stone (1977) 3 FPPC Ops. 52


Our File No. A-90-284
Page 3
(copy enclosed). Resolutions were adopted with respect to the
funds received from Transrapid, Alsthom, and the French government
prior to payment of the travel expenses - You have submitted cop-
ies of these resolutions with your letter requesting advice.
There are eight california cornmissioners on the Super speed
commission. one conmissioner is an appointed member of another
state commission; three commissioners are from the private sector;
and four are state and local elected officers.
ANALYSIS
1.
3. The donor has not linited use of the gift
to specif j.ed or high 1evel emPloyees, but rather
has made it generalLy available to agency personnel
in connection with agency business without regard
to official 
status; and
4. The naking and use of the gift was formal-
ized in a resolution of the public entity ... which
enbodies the standards set forth above.
In re Stone′  supra′  3 FPPC Ops.
at 57.
the comrnissioners?
You wish to know if the paynents of travel and accornmodation
expenses are reportable gifts to the commissj-oners or are gifts to
thi super Speed counission. If the comrnissioners have received
gifts ialued at $250 or more during the preceding 12 nontls-, they
iould have to disqualify themselves from any.decisions which will
have a reasonably foreseeabl-e material financial effect on the
donors which is iistinguistrable from the effect on the public
generally. 
(Section 87103(e).) Gifts of $50 or more are
ieportabie on financial disclosure statements. (Section
87207 (a) (1) . )
In fn re Stone, suDra, the Comnission concl-uded that in
certain sitttati"ns, a gift may be made to a public entity without
providing any significint 
or unusual benefit to the public of-
ii"i.f. 
-Under such circumstances, the public official 
has no
reportinq obligations since any benefit he or she receives,
allhough - free 5f charge to the official 
and to the publj-c entity,
rwould be analogous to reiubursement for expenses or per dien from
a state or locaL government agency, items which are not report-
able. Section 82030(b) (2).ir (In re Stoner SllgE!! r at 57') 
The
Commission stated that to be deemed a gift to the public entity,
the gift should satisfy at least the follouing four criteria:
1. The donor intended to donate the gift to
the public entity and not to the official;
2- The public entity exercises substantial
control over use of the gifti


Our Fi■e No. A-90-284
Page 4
If the gift of travel expenses and accoDmodations is deerned a
gift to the Super speed conmission, the conmissioners have no
reporting requirements since, as in the Stone opinion, any benefit
they receive v/ould be analogous to rej.mbursenent for expenses or
per diern from the super speed Commission. Such items are excLuded
from reporting under section 82030(b) (2). 
Since such items are
not gifts or incorne to the commissioners, they do not give rise to
any disgualification obligation under Sections 87100 and 87103.
According to the facts that you have provj.ded, the super
Speed Cornrnission has adopted a resolution fonnalizing the gifts by
Tlansrapid, Alsthon and the French government to the Super sPeed
co-.iss-ion of travel expenses, including transportation, accom-
modations and meaIs. The resolutions that you subnitted state
that the gift is to the super Speed comrnission; that the Super
speed cornraission will exercise substantial control over the use of
the gift; 
and that the use of. the gift is not litnited to any
specified high-Ieve1 officials, 
but is generally available to the
pirsonnel of the super Speed Commission in connection until the
business of the cornmission.
Based upon the f oregroing, the gifts of transportation, accorn-
rnodations and meal,s from Transrapid, Alsthon and the French
government would be deemed gifts to the super Speed coEmission
iather than gifts to the coranissioners. Therefore, the commis-
sioners do not have to rePort these exPenses when filing state-
ments of Economic Interest.
However, no sucil. resolution has been adopted with respect to
the expenses paid by Bechtel for hotels, meals and ground
transportation j.n Enqland. The fact that Bechtel is engaged in a
joint venture with Transrapid is not sufficj.ent to bring Bechtel-
witfrin the uEbrella of the resolution covering Transrapid.
Furthermore, the resoLution presented concerning the accePtance of
the gift from Transrapid is specifically directed at travel
expenses for the travel in Germany. The resolution does not cover
the tour of facilities 
in England. Therefore, the expenses for
hotels, roeals and ground transPortation paid by Bechtel for each
comnissioner in England Dust be reported as qifts to the conmis-
sioners.
2. Must disclosure statenents be filed bv all California connis-
si,oners or onl-y those wio are elected offiqers?
You have indicated that the Super Speed Commission is in the
process of adopting a conflict of interest code. All persons who
are classified as itdesignated eEployeesrr nust file Statements of
Econornic Interest within 30 days of the effective date of the
code. (Section 87302(b).) The Act defines I'desigrnated employeetr
as follows:
ttDesignated employeetr neans any officer,


Our Fi■e No. A-90-284
Page 5
employee, member or consultant of any agency whose
position rrith the agency;
(a) Is exempt from the state civil service system
by virtue of subdivision (a), (c), (d), (e) , (f) ,
(S), or (n) of section 4 of Article vrr of the
Constitution, unless the position is electj.ve or
so1e1y secretarial, clerical or manuaL;
(b) Is elective, other than an eLective state
of f ice r'
(c) Is designated in a Conflict of Interest code
because the position entails the nakinq or
participation in the naking of decisions which nay
ioreseeably have a naterial effect on any financial
. 
intarest.
(d) Is involved as a state employee at other than
a cleri.caI or ninisterial 
level in the functions of
negotiating or signing any contract arrarded through
conpetitive bidding, in naking decisions in
conjunction with the cornpetitive bidding process,
or in negotiatinq, signing, or naking decisions on
contracts executed pursuant to Section 10122 of the
Public contract code.
'rDesignated employeert does not include an elected
state officer, any unsalaried meuber of any board
or coumission which serves a solely advisory
function, any public official 
specified in section
a72oo, and also does not include any unsalaried
member of a nonregrulatory cornmj.ttee, section,
commission or other such entity of the State Bar of
California.
section 82019.
Conmissioners who are nembers of the Legislature are rrelected
state officersrr. 
As such, they do not have to file disclosure
statements as designated enployees under the conflict of interest
code of the Super Speed Conmiss j.on. However, they file-Statements
of Economic Interest under other provisions of the Act.z other
members of the super Speed Commission who are specified in Section
a72oo of the Act and file Statements of Economic Interest are also
excLuded fron filing pursuant to the conflict of interest code.
These conmissioners who are excluded because they file under other
provisions of the Act should disclose the gifts from Bechtel on
the Statements of Econonic Interest they currently fi1e.
sections 87200 - 872■
0.


Our Fi■e No. A-90-284
Page 6
SH: MWE : aa
Enc losure
I trust that this provides you with the guidance that you
need.  If you have any additiona■
 questions, p■ease contact me at
(9■6)322-590■
.
Sincere■y′
Scott Ha■■abrin
A瘍
秒〃兆
By: l,largaret W. Ellison
Counsel, Legal Division
